title: Amanda Brown (musician)

infobox.value: Amanda Gabrielle Brown   (1965-11-17) 17 November 1965 (age 60)  [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), New South Wales, Australia

* Vitamin * Fuse/Think * [Polydor](/wiki/Polydor "Polydor") * [rooART](/wiki/RooART "RooART") * [EMI](/wiki/EMI "EMI") * [Capitol](/wiki/Capitol_Records "Capitol Records") * [Beggars Banquet](/wiki/Beggars_Banquet_Records "Beggars Banquet Records")

(born 17 November 1965) is an Australian composer, multi-instrumentalist, singer and songwriter. She was the violinist of Australian indie rock band [the Go-Betweens](/wiki/The_Go-Betweens "The Go-Betweens") from 1986 to 1989 and recorded on their studio albums *[Tallulah](/wiki/Tallulah_(The_Go-Betweens_album) "Tallulah (The Go-Betweens album)")* (1987) and *[16 Lovers Lane](/wiki/16_Lovers_Lane "16 Lovers Lane")* (1988). Brown has also worked as a [session musician](/wiki/Session_musician "Session musician") and, since 2000, as a screen music composer. She won the [AACTA Award for Best Original Music Score](/wiki/AACTA_Award_for_Best_Original_Music_Score "AACTA Award for Best Original Music Score") in [2020](/wiki/10th_AACTA_Awards "10th AACTA Awards") for *[Babyteeth](/wiki/Babyteeth_(film) "Babyteeth (film)")* (2019) and also Best Original Music Score in a Documentary for *[Brazen Hussies](/wiki/Brazen_Hussies_(2020_film) "Brazen Hussies (2020 film)")* (2020). At the [APRA-AGSC Screen Music Awards of 2009](/wiki/APRA_Music_Awards_of_2009 "APRA Music Awards of 2009") she won Best Music for a Documentary for *[Sidney Nolan: Mask and Memory](/wiki/Sidney_Nolan "Sidney Nolan")* (2008) and Best Music for a Television Series or Serial for *[The Secrets She Keeps](/wiki/The_Secrets_She_Keeps "The Secrets She Keeps")* at the [2020 ceremony](/wiki/APRA_Music_Awards_of_2020 "APRA Music Awards of 2020").

first_paragraph: **Amanda Gabrielle Brown** (born 17 November 1965) is an Australian composer, multi-instrumentalist, singer and songwriter. She was the violinist of Australian indie rock band [the Go-Betweens](/wiki/The_Go-Betweens "The Go-Betweens") from 1986 to 1989 and recorded on their studio albums *[Tallulah](/wiki/Tallulah_(The_Go-Betweens_album) "Tallulah (The Go-Betweens album)")* (1987) and *[16 Lovers Lane](/wiki/16_Lovers_Lane "16 Lovers Lane")* (1988). Brown has also worked as a [session musician](/wiki/Session_musician "Session musician") and, since 2000, as a screen music composer. She won the [AACTA Award for Best Original Music Score](/wiki/AACTA_Award_for_Best_Original_Music_Score "AACTA Award for Best Original Music Score") in [2020](/wiki/10th_AACTA_Awards "10th AACTA Awards") for *[Babyteeth](/wiki/Babyteeth_(film) "Babyteeth (film)")* (2019) and also Best Original Music Score in a Documentary for *[Brazen Hussies](/wiki/Brazen_Hussies_(2020_film) "Brazen Hussies (2020 film)")* (2020). At the [APRA-AGSC Screen Music Awards of 2009](/wiki/APRA_Music_Awards_of_2009 "APRA Music Awards of 2009") she won Best Music for a Documentary for *[Sidney Nolan: Mask and Memory](/wiki/Sidney_Nolan "Sidney Nolan")* (2008) and Best Music for a Television Series or Serial for *[The Secrets She Keeps](/wiki/The_Secrets_She_Keeps "The Secrets She Keeps")* at the [2020 ceremony](/wiki/APRA_Music_Awards_of_2020 "APRA Music Awards of 2020").

Musical artist

**Amanda Gabrielle Brown** (born 17 November 1965) is an Australian composer, multi-instrumentalist, singer and songwriter. She was the violinist of Australian indie rock band [the Go-Betweens](/wiki/The_Go-Betweens "The Go-Betweens") from 1986 to 1989 and recorded on their studio albums *[Tallulah](/wiki/Tallulah_(The_Go-Betweens_album) "Tallulah (The Go-Betweens album)")* (1987) and *[16 Lovers Lane](/wiki/16_Lovers_Lane "16 Lovers Lane")* (1988). Brown has also worked as a [session musician](/wiki/Session_musician "Session musician") and, since 2000, as a screen music composer. She won the [AACTA Award for Best Original Music Score](/wiki/AACTA_Award_for_Best_Original_Music_Score "AACTA Award for Best Original Music Score") in [2020](/wiki/10th_AACTA_Awards "10th AACTA Awards") for *[Babyteeth](/wiki/Babyteeth_(film) "Babyteeth (film)")* (2019) and also Best Original Music Score in a Documentary for *[Brazen Hussies](/wiki/Brazen_Hussies_(2020_film) "Brazen Hussies (2020 film)")* (2020). At the [APRA-AGSC Screen Music Awards of 2009](/wiki/APRA_Music_Awards_of_2009 "APRA Music Awards of 2009") she won Best Music for a Documentary for *[Sidney Nolan: Mask and Memory](/wiki/Sidney_Nolan "Sidney Nolan")* (2008) and Best Music for a Television Series or Serial for *[The Secrets She Keeps](/wiki/The_Secrets_She_Keeps "The Secrets She Keeps")* at the [2020 ceremony](/wiki/APRA_Music_Awards_of_2020 "APRA Music Awards of 2020").

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Amanda_Brown_(musician)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Amanda Gabrielle Brown was born on 17 November 1965.[[1]](#cite_note-APRA_1973-1)[[2]](#cite_note-AGSC_Bio-2) For secondary education she attended [Sydney Girls High School](/wiki/Sydney_Girls_High_School "Sydney Girls High School") where she took [violin](/wiki/Violin "Violin") lessons.[[3]](#cite_note-3) She received training in classical ballet.[[2]](#cite_note-AGSC_Bio-2) She started a course in archaeology and ancient history at the [University of Sydney](/wiki/University_of_Sydney "University of Sydney") but left before completion to pursue her music career.[[2]](#cite_note-AGSC_Bio-2) Brown is a graduate of the [Sydney Conservatorium of Music](/wiki/Sydney_Conservatorium_of_Music "Sydney Conservatorium of Music") (1995), [University of Western Sydney](/wiki/Western_Sydney_University "Western Sydney University") ([BMus](/wiki/Bachelor_of_Music "Bachelor of Music"), 1997) and [Australian Film Television and Radio School](/wiki/Australian_Film_Television_and_Radio_School "Australian Film Television and Radio School") (Screen Composition, 2000).[[4]](#cite_note-vitamin-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Mana_Mus-5)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Amanda_Brown_(musician)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Amanda_Brown_(musician)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1980s")]

Main article: [The Go-Betweens](/wiki/The_Go-Betweens "The Go-Betweens")

From 1983 Brown on violin, [oboe](/wiki/Oboe "Oboe") and vocals was a member of jazz rock group Climbing Frame in Sydney, which issued a self-titled five-track extended play in 1984.[[6]](#cite_note-Holmgren_AB-6) Other members were Peter Brookes on drums, David Hawkes on [clarinet](/wiki/Clarinet "Clarinet") and acoustic guitar, Neil Hawkes on saxophone, Michael Sherman on keyboards and trumpet, Barry Wolifson on oboe and Jon Wright on bass and electric guitar. Brown's other early bands during the 1980s were Tender Mercies (with [John Willsteed](/wiki/John_Willsteed "John Willsteed") and Michael O'Connell) and Blood Brothers (with O'Connell),[[6]](#cite_note-Holmgren_AB-6) in which she played violin, oboe, guitar and keyboards.

By March 1986 Brown had joined Australian indie rock group [the Go-Betweens](/wiki/The_Go-Betweens "The Go-Betweens") for Australian tour dates after being discovered playing live in a café.[[7]](#cite_note-Riddle-7)[[8]](#cite_note-Sutton-8) The group had formed in [Brisbane](/wiki/Brisbane "Brisbane") in 1977 and in 1986 they comprised [Robert Forster](/wiki/Robert_Forster_(musician) "Robert Forster (musician)") on lead vocals and guitar, [Grant McLennan](/wiki/Grant_McLennan "Grant McLennan") on lead vocals, guitar and harmonica, [Lindy Morrison](/wiki/Lindy_Morrison "Lindy Morrison") on drums and backing vocals and [Robert Vickers](/wiki/Robert_Vickers "Robert Vickers") on bass guitar.[[9]](#cite_note-McFarlane_tG-B-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Holmgren_tG-B-10) The group had signed a new recording contract with [Beggars Banquet](/wiki/Beggars_Banquet_Records "Beggars Banquet Records"). Later that year Brown officially joined the band, which was then based in London, to provide backing vocals, violin, oboe, guitar, keyboards and arrangements.[[9]](#cite_note-McFarlane_tG-B-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Holmgren_tG-B-10)[[11]](#cite_note-Strong-11) Forster recalled, "She was a girl we saw in Sydney, a multi-instrumentalist, and she sings; we thought we needed that."[[8]](#cite_note-Sutton-8)

Brown was recorded on two of the Go-Betweens' studio albums, *[Tallulah](/wiki/Tallulah_(The_Go-Betweens_album) "Tallulah (The Go-Betweens album)")* (1987) and *[16 Lovers Lane](/wiki/16_Lovers_Lane "16 Lovers Lane")* (1988).[[9]](#cite_note-McFarlane_tG-B-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Holmgren_tG-B-10) The five-piece ensemble returned to Australia in November 1987, where Vickers was replaced by Brown's former bandmate Willsteed on bass guitar.[[9]](#cite_note-McFarlane_tG-B-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Holmgren_tG-B-10) The latter album provided the group's most popular single, "[Streets of Your Town](/wiki/Streets_of_Your_Town "Streets of Your Town")" (July 1988), which was written by McLennan while living with Brown in Sydney.[[9]](#cite_note-McFarlane_tG-B-9)[[12]](#cite_note-Stafford-12) [Maura Johnston](/wiki/Maura_Johnston "Maura Johnston") of [NPR](/wiki/NPR "NPR") observed, "The shimmering 'Streets of Your Town', a saudade-infused saunter that's highlighted by Brown's sun-dappled backing vocal, is an especially summery offering."[[13]](#cite_note-Johnston-13) The band toured for 18 months promoting *16 Lovers Lane*, which ended in Munich, Germany. [Michael Armiger](/wiki/Michael_Armiger "Michael Armiger") replaced Willsteed on bass guitar in March 1989.[[9]](#cite_note-McFarlane_tG-B-9)[[10]](#cite_note-Holmgren_tG-B-10) The Go-Betweens broke up in December 1989,[[9]](#cite_note-McFarlane_tG-B-9) after a farewell tour of England.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

As a session musician, Brown was recorded on [Love Gone Wrong](/wiki/John_Kennedy_(Australian_musician)#John_Kennedy's_Love_Gone_Wrong "John Kennedy (Australian musician)")'s album *From Go to Woe* (1986) providing violin, including on their single "Big Country" (July).[[6]](#cite_note-Holmgren_AB-6)[[14]](#cite_note-Coupe-14) Former Climbing Frame bandmates Brookes, David Hawkes and Sherman were members of Lucky Dinosaurs in 1987 with Lea Cameron on lead vocals, Adam Cox on guitar, Greg Flannery on saxophone, Lindsay Jones on trombone and David Richardson on bass guitar. Brown provided bass guitar, drums, guitar and keyboards for Lucky Dinosaurs' four-track EP *That Was* (1987).[[15]](#cite_note-Tharunka_1-15) During the 1980s she also worked on [Chad's Tree](/wiki/Chad%27s_Tree "Chad's Tree")'s album *Buckle in the Rail* (1987) and [the Reels](/wiki/The_Reels "The Reels")'s album *[Neighbors](/wiki/Neighbors_(album) "Neighbors (album)")* (1988).[[6]](#cite_note-Holmgren_AB-6)

### 1990s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Amanda_Brown_(musician)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1990s")]

Following the break-up of the Go-Betweens, Brown and Morrison formed the pop band Cleopatra Wong (1990–1993) in Sydney.[[11]](#cite_note-Strong-11)[[16]](#cite_note-McFarlane_CW-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Holmgren_CW-17) They released two EPs on the [rooArt](/wiki/RooArt "RooArt") label, six-track *Egg* (February 1992) and five-track *Cleopatra's Lament* (March 1993), including the single (and video) "Thank You".[[16]](#cite_note-McFarlane_CW-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Holmgren_CW-17) Besides violin and keyboards Brown provided lead vocals, [mandolin](/wiki/Mandolin "Mandolin") and acoustic guitar; together with Morrison, she co-wrote their material.[[16]](#cite_note-McFarlane_CW-16)[[18]](#cite_note-APRA_Cleopatra-18) For *Egg* they were joined in the studio by former bandmate Armiger on bass guitar, Colin Bloxsom on percussion (ex-Love Gone Wrong), Frank Millward on keyboards and Mark Moffatt on guitars as well as producer (ex-[the Monitors](/wiki/The_Monitors_(Australian_band) "The Monitors (Australian band)")).[[16]](#cite_note-McFarlane_CW-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Holmgren_CW-17) On *Cleopatra's Lament* the duo "use striking imagery or constructions in their lyrics and complex arrangements in their music, and are unafraid to be playful or delight in the unexpected... [her] skills on violin, guitar, oboe and mandolin provide a richer, more sophisticated sound".[[19]](#cite_note-Lament-19)

Brown was the first musician to guest host the ABC-TV music video programme, *[rage](/wiki/Rage_(TV_program) "Rage (TV program)")*, on 7 January 1990.[[20]](#cite_note-Rage_Host-20)[[21]](#cite_note-Rage_Host_2-21) During the early 1990s she undertook session work on [Tactics](/wiki/Tactics_(band) "Tactics (band)")' *The Great Gusto* (1990), [Bughouse](/wiki/Bughouse_(band) "Bughouse (band)")'s *Every Fool in Town* (1991), [Shane Howard](/wiki/Shane_Howard "Shane Howard")'s "Escape from Reality" (single, 1991), McLennan's *[Watershed](/wiki/Watershed_(Grant_McLennan_album) "Watershed (Grant McLennan album)")* (1991), Died Pretty's *[Doughboy Hollow](/wiki/Doughboy_Hollow "Doughboy Hollow")* (1991), Wendy Matthews's *[Lily](/wiki/Lily_(Wendy_Matthews_album) "Lily (Wendy Matthews album)")* (1992) and Wayne Gillespie's *Living in Exile* (1993).[[6]](#cite_note-Holmgren_AB-6) Later work in the decade included playing with Sydney band Love Me (1997–1998) and sessions for Boxcar's *[Algorhythm](/wiki/Algorhythm_(album) "Algorhythm (album)")* (1994), [R.E.M.](/wiki/R.E.M._(band) "R.E.M. (band)"), [Dog Trumpet](/wiki/Dog_Trumpet "Dog Trumpet")'s *Suitcase* (1996), Silverchair's *[Freak Show](/wiki/Freak_Show_(album) "Freak Show (album)")* (1997), [David Lane](/wiki/David_Lane_(musician) "David Lane (musician)"), the Cruel Sea's *[Over Easy](/wiki/Over_Easy "Over Easy")* (1998) and [the Apartments](/wiki/The_Apartments "The Apartments").[[6]](#cite_note-Holmgren_AB-6)[[22]](#cite_note-go-betweens.org.uk-22)

### 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Amanda_Brown_(musician)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2000s")]

Brown has established a career as a screen composer, writing for television and film soundtracks since 2000.[[23]](#cite_note-AMC_Bio-23) In 2003 she released the *Incognita* soundtrack album via Vitamin Records,[[6]](#cite_note-Holmgren_AB-6)[[22]](#cite_note-go-betweens.org.uk-22) which provided a mixture of songs and instrumentals accompanying performances of the contemporary dance work of the same name by the Stalker Theatre Company.[[24]](#cite_note-Incognita_1-24)[[4]](#cite_note-vitamin-4) *Incognita* toured Australia and Europe, commencing at the [Sydney Festival](/wiki/Sydney_Festival "Sydney Festival") 2003; the show explored issues of Australia's past and present.[[24]](#cite_note-Incognita_1-24) Accompanying Brown on the soundtrack are Richard Boxhall on guitar, Jim Elliot on drums and percussion, Ken Gormley on bass guitar, Maryann Camilleri on vocals ("In My Heart's a Home for You") and Kimi Tupaiya on vocals ("Hey Devil").[[25]](#cite_note-NLA_Incognita-25)

Brown was also the occasional sixth member of [Toni Collette](/wiki/Toni_Collette "Toni Collette")'s live band, [The Finish](/wiki/Toni_Collette_%26_the_Finish "Toni Collette & the Finish"), and has continued to record and perform with various artists including [David Bridie](/wiki/David_Bridie "David Bridie"), [The Vines](/wiki/The_Vines_(band) "The Vines (band)"), [Youth Group](/wiki/Youth_Group "Youth Group") and [Josh Pyke](/wiki/Josh_Pyke "Josh Pyke").[[6]](#cite_note-Holmgren_AB-6)[[22]](#cite_note-go-betweens.org.uk-22) In the winter 2007–08 she duetted with the Danish singer Michael Møller on the song "A Sunday Routine" from his debut solo outing from his band [Moi Caprice](/wiki/Moi_Caprice "Moi Caprice").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In 2008 Brown won the IF Inside Film Award for Best Music for her score of *Son of a Lion*.[[27]](#cite_note-27) A soundtrack album was released in late 2008.[[28]](#cite_note-28) In [2009](/wiki/APRA_Awards_of_2009 "APRA Awards of 2009") she won the APRA/Australian Guild of Screen Composers Award for Best Music in a Documentary for the score of *Sidney Nolan: Mask and Memory*.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

Brown continued to diversify; in 2009 she mixed David Lane's album *Head in the Clouds*.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

Brown has composed scores for [feature films](/wiki/Feature_film "Feature film"), including *[Preservation](/w/index.php?title=Preservation_(2003_film)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Preservation (2003 film) (page does not exist)")* (2003), *[Floodhouse](/w/index.php?title=Floodhouse&action=edit&redlink=1 "Floodhouse (page does not exist)")* (2003), *[Look Both Ways](/wiki/Look_Both_Ways_(2005_film) "Look Both Ways (2005 film)")* (2005) *[Monkey Puzzle](/wiki/Monkey_Puzzle_(film) "Monkey Puzzle (film)")*  (2007) and *[Son of a Lion](/wiki/Son_of_a_Lion "Son of a Lion")* (2008). Documentaries she has composed the music for include *Suburb For Sale* (2006) and *A Well Founded Fear* (2008).[[31]](#cite_note-imdb.com-31)

### 2010s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Amanda_Brown_(musician)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2010s")]

In 2020 Brown composed the soundtrack for the documentary *[Brazen Hussies](/wiki/Brazen_Hussies_(documentary) "Brazen Hussies (documentary)").* As of January 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Amanda_Brown_(musician)&action=edit) she was recording her first-ever solo album of original material.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

## Other roles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Amanda_Brown_(musician)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Other roles")]

In 2015, Brown was elected to the [APRA](/wiki/Australasian_Performing_Right_Association "Australasian Performing Right Association") Board as a writer/director.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Amanda_Brown_(musician)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Brown established a personal relationship with the Go-Betweens' founder [Grant McLennan](/wiki/Grant_McLennan "Grant McLennan") a few months after she joined the band in 1986, which ended before the group disbanded in 1990.[[34]](#cite_note-Kingsmill-34)[[35]](#cite_note-Beriyant-35) McLennan wrote "Streets of Your Town" (1988) while living in Sydney with Brown, who provided backing vocals, including the prominent "shine" refrain.[[12]](#cite_note-Stafford-12) By May 1993, Brown was dating radio and TV personality [Simon Marnie](/wiki/Simon_Marnie "Simon Marnie").[[36]](#cite_note-Lang-36) As of August 2015[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Amanda_Brown_(musician)&action=edit), the couple were living in the Sydney suburb of Maroubra, New South Wales.[[37]](#cite_note-37)
